[Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") is the second-largest religion in [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") after [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), with more than 650 million [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") living there, forming about one-third of the region's population. Islam first spread along the coastal regions of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), almost as soon as it started in the [Arabian Peninsula](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula"), as the Arab traders brought it to South Asia. South Asia has the [largest population](/wiki/Islam_by_country#Continents "Islam by country") of Muslims in the world, with about one-third of all Muslims living here.[[17]](#cite_note-auto1-17)[[18]](#cite_note-auto-18) Islam is the dominant religion in half of the South Asian countries (Pakistan, Maldives, Bangladesh and Afghanistan). It is the [second largest](/wiki/Islam_in_India "Islam in India") religion in India and [third largest](/wiki/South_Asia#Religions "South Asia") in Sri Lanka and [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal").

On the Indian subcontinent, Islam first appeared in the southwestern tip of the peninsula, in today's [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") state. Arabs traded with [Malabar](/wiki/Malabar_(Northern_Kerala) "Malabar (Northern Kerala)") even before the birth of [Prophet Muhammad](/wiki/Prophet_Muhammad "Prophet Muhammad"). Native legends say that a group of [Sahaba](/wiki/Sahaba "Sahaba"), under [Malik Ibn Deenar](/wiki/Malik_Bin_Deenar "Malik Bin Deenar"), arrived on the [Malabar Coast](/wiki/Malabar_Coast "Malabar Coast") and preached Islam. Local kings who converted to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") existed in places such as the [Western Coastal Plains](/wiki/Western_Coastal_Plains "Western Coastal Plains") as early as the 7th century. According to that legend, [the first mosque of India](/wiki/Cheraman_Juma_Masjid "Cheraman Juma Masjid") was built by the mandate of the last King of [Chera Perumals of Makotai](/wiki/Chera_Perumals_of_Makotai "Chera Perumals of Makotai"), who accepted Islam and received the name *Tajudheen* during the lifetime of the Islamic prophet [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") (c. 570–632).[[19]](#cite_note-BT-19)[[20]](#cite_note-Fihrist-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) On a similar note, [Malabar Muslims](/wiki/Malabar_Muslims "Malabar Muslims") on the western coast also claim that they converted to Islam in Muhammad's lifetime. According to *[Qissat Shakarwati Farmad](/wiki/Qissat_Shakarwati_Farmad "Qissat Shakarwati Farmad")*, the *[Masjids](/wiki/Masjids "Masjids")* at Kodungallur, [Kollam](/wiki/Kollam "Kollam"), [Madayi](/wiki/Madayi "Madayi"), [Barkur](/wiki/Barkur "Barkur"), [Mangalore](/wiki/Mangalore "Mangalore"), [Kasaragod](/wiki/Kasaragod "Kasaragod"), [Kannur](/wiki/Kannur "Kannur"), [Dharmadam](/wiki/Dharmadam "Dharmadam"), [Panthalayini](/wiki/Koyilandy "Koyilandy"), and [Chaliyam](/wiki/Chaliyam "Chaliyam"), were built during the era of Malik Dinar, and they are among the oldest *Masjid*s (mosques) in the Indian Subcontinent.[[22]](#cite_note-auto2-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Kumar_2012,_P_444-23) [[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalf20091-24) Historicaly, the Barwada Mosque in [Ghogha](/wiki/Ghogha "Ghogha"), Gujarat built before 623 CE, [Cheraman Juma Mosque](/wiki/Cheraman_Juma_Mosque "Cheraman Juma Mosque") (629 CE) in [Methala](/wiki/Methala "Methala"), Kerala and [Palaiya Jumma Palli](/wiki/Palaiya_Jumma_Palli "Palaiya Jumma Palli") (630 CE) in [Kilakarai](/wiki/Kilakarai "Kilakarai"), Tamil Nadu are three of [the first mosques in South Asia](/wiki/List_of_mosques_in_India "List of mosques in India").[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[23]](#cite_note-Kumar_2012,_P_444-23)

The first incursion occurred through sea by Caliph [Umar](/wiki/Umar "Umar")'s governor of [Bahrain](/wiki/Bahrain "Bahrain"), Usman ibn Abu al-Aas, who sent his brother Hakam ibn Abu al-Aas to raid and [reconnoitre](/wiki/Reconnoitre "Reconnoitre") the [Makran](/wiki/Makran "Makran") region[[29]](#cite_note-29) around 636 CE or 643 AD long before any [Arab army](/wiki/Rashidun_army "Rashidun army") reached the frontier of India by land. Al-Hakim ibn Jabalah al-Abdi, who attacked Makran in the year 649 AD, was an early partisan of [Ali ibn Abu Talib](/wiki/Ali_ibn_Abu_Talib "Ali ibn Abu Talib").[[30]](#cite_note-MacLean,_Derryl_N._1989_pp._126-30) During the caliphate of Ali, many [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") [Jats](/wiki/Jats "Jats") of Sindh had come under the influence of [Shi'ism](/wiki/Shi%27ism "Shi'ism")[[31]](#cite_note-31) and some even participated in the [Battle of Camel](/wiki/Battle_of_Camel "Battle of Camel") and died fighting for Ali.[[30]](#cite_note-MacLean,_Derryl_N._1989_pp._126-30) According to popular tradition, Islam was brought to [Lakshadweep](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep") islands, situated just to the west of Malabar Coast, by [Ubaidullah](/wiki/Sheikh_Ubaidullah "Sheikh Ubaidullah") in 661 CE.

## Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_South_Asia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origins")]

Main articles: [Muhammad in Islam](/wiki/Muhammad_in_Islam "Muhammad in Islam"), [Rashidun Caliphate](/wiki/Rashidun_Caliphate "Rashidun Caliphate"), and [Spread of Islam](/wiki/Spread_of_Islam "Spread of Islam")

Islamic influence first came to be felt in the Indian subcontinent during the early 7th century with the advent of [Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") traders. Arab traders used to visit the [Malabar region](/wiki/Malabar_region "Malabar region") to trade even before Islam had been established in Arabia. Unlike the coasts of Malabar, the northwestern coasts were not as receptive to the Middle Eastern arrivals. Hindu merchants in [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh") and [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat") perceived the Arab merchants to be competitors.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Periplous_of_the_Erythraean_Sea.svg/250px-Periplous_of_the_Erythraean_Sea.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Periplous_of_the_Erythraean_Sea.svg)

Names, routes and locations of the *[Periplus of the Erythraean Sea](/wiki/Periplus_of_the_Erythraean_Sea "Periplus of the Erythraean Sea")* (1st century CE)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Cheraman_jumamasjid.JPG/250px-Cheraman_jumamasjid.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cheraman_jumamasjid.JPG)

[Cheraman Perumal Juma Masjid](/wiki/Cheraman_Juma_Masjid "Cheraman Juma Masjid") on the Malabar Coast, probably the first Mosque in India

Trade relations have existed between Arabia and the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") since ancient times. Even in the [pre-Islamic era](/wiki/Pre-Islamic_Arabia "Pre-Islamic Arabia"), Arab traders used to visit the [Konkan](/wiki/Konkan "Konkan")-[Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat") coast and [Malabar Coast](/wiki/Malabar_Coast "Malabar Coast"), which linked them with the ports of [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"). Newly Islamised Arabs were Islam's first contact with India. Historians Elliot and Dowson say in their book *[The History of India, as Told by Its Own Historians](/wiki/The_History_of_India,_as_Told_by_Its_Own_Historians "The History of India, as Told by Its Own Historians")*, that the first ship bearing Muslim travellers was seen on the Indian coast as early as 630 CE. H. G. Rawlinson in his book *Ancient and Medieval History of India*[[32]](#cite_note-32) claims that the first [Arab Muslims](/wiki/Arab_Muslims "Arab Muslims") settled on the Indian coast in the last part of the 7th century CE. This fact is corroborated by J. Sturrock in his *Madras District Manuals*[[33]](#cite_note-33) and by Haridas Bhattacharya in *Cultural Heritage of India Vol. IV*.[[34]](#cite_note-34) With the rise of Islam, Arabs emerged as a significant cultural force on the global stage. Through their extensive trade and commerce networks, Arab merchants and traders became key ambassadors of the faith, shared its teachings wherever they traveled.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

According to popular tradition, [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") was brought to [Lakshadweep](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep") islands, situated just to the west of [Malabar Coast](/wiki/Malabar_Coast "Malabar Coast"), by [Ubaidullah](/wiki/Sheikh_Ubaidullah "Sheikh Ubaidullah") in 661 CE. His grave is believed to be located on the island of [Andrott](/wiki/Andrott "Andrott").[[36]](#cite_note-36) According to [Kerala Muslim](/wiki/Malabar_Muslims "Malabar Muslims") tradition, the [Masjid Zeenath Baksh](/wiki/Masjid_Zeenath_Baksh "Masjid Zeenath Baksh") at [Mangalore](/wiki/Mangalore "Mangalore") is one of the oldest mosques in the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent").[[37]](#cite_note-37) According to the [Legend of Cheraman Perumals](/wiki/Legend_of_Cheraman_Perumals "Legend of Cheraman Perumals"), the first Indian mosque was built in 624 CE at [Kodungallur](/wiki/Kodungallur "Kodungallur") in present-day [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") with the mandate of the last the ruler (the Cheraman Perumal) of [Chera dynasty](/wiki/Chera_dynasty "Chera dynasty"), who converted to Islam during the lifetime of the [Islamic prophet](/wiki/Prophets_and_messengers_in_Islam "Prophets and messengers in Islam") [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") (c. 570–632).[[38]](#cite_note-SimpsonKresse2008-38)[[39]](#cite_note-Kupferschmidt1987-39)[[40]](#cite_note-Raṇṭattāṇi2007-40) According to *[Qissat Shakarwati Farmad](/wiki/Qissat_Shakarwati_Farmad "Qissat Shakarwati Farmad")*, the [*Masjids*](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque") at [Kodungallur](/wiki/Kodungallur "Kodungallur"), [Kollam](/wiki/Kollam "Kollam"), [Madayi](/wiki/Madayi "Madayi"), [Barkur](/wiki/Barkur "Barkur"), [Mangalore](/wiki/Mangalore "Mangalore"), [Kasaragod](/wiki/Kasaragod "Kasaragod"), [Kannur](/wiki/Kannur "Kannur"), [Dharmadam](/wiki/Dharmadam "Dharmadam"), [Panthalayini](/wiki/Koyilandy "Koyilandy"), and [Chaliyam](/wiki/Chaliyam "Chaliyam"), were built during the era of [Malik Dinar](/wiki/Malik_Dinar "Malik Dinar"), and they are among the oldest *Masjid*s in the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent").[[22]](#cite_note-auto2-22) It is believed that [Malik Dinar](/wiki/Malik_Dinar "Malik Dinar") died at [Thalangara](/wiki/Thalangara "Thalangara") in [Kasaragod](/wiki/Kasaragod "Kasaragod") town.[[41]](#cite_note-ch-41)

The first Indian [mosque](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque"), [Cheraman Juma Mosque](/wiki/Cheraman_Juma_Mosque "Cheraman Juma Mosque"), is thought to have been built in 629 CE by [Malik Deenar](/wiki/Malik_Deenar "Malik Deenar")[[42]](#cite_note-42) although some historians say the first mosque was in [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat") in between 610 and 623 CE.[[43]](#cite_note-43) In [Malabar](/wiki/Malabar_region "Malabar region"), the [Mappilas](/wiki/Malabar_Muslims "Malabar Muslims") may have been the first community to convert to Islam.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Historical evidence shows that Arabs and Muslims interacted with Indians from the early days of Islam and possibly before the arrival of Islam in Arab regions. Arab traders transmitted the [numeral system developed by Indians](/wiki/Hindu%E2%80%93Arabic_numeral_system "Hindu–Arabic numeral system") to the Middle East and Europe.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Commercial intercourse between Arabia and India had gone on from time immemorial, with for example the sale of dates and aromatic herbs by Arabs traders who came to Indian shores every spring with the advent of the [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") breeze. People living on the western coast of India were as familiar with the annual coming of Arab traders as they were with the flocks of monsoon birds; they were as ancient a phenomenon as the monsoon itself. However, whereas monsoon birds flew back to Africa after a sojourn of few months, not all traders returned to their homes in the desert; many married Indian women and settled in India.[[46]](#cite_note-A_history_of_the_Sikhs-p20-46)

The advent of [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") (569–632 CE) transformed the previously [idolatrous](/wiki/Religion_in_pre-Islamic_Arabia "Religion in pre-Islamic Arabia") and fragmented Arabs into a nation unified by faith and driven by a shared commitment to spreading the message of Islam. Arab merchant seamen, who had long brought goods like dates to South India, now introduced the new religion, which found a warm reception in the region. [South Indian](/wiki/South_India "South India") communities welcomed the construction of mosques and facilitated cultural integration, including intermarriage between Arabs and local women. This led to the formation of a distinct Indian-Arabian Muslim community. By the early 9th century, Muslim missionaries in Malabar achieved a significant milestone when they inspired the conversion of the local king to Islam.[[46]](#cite_note-A_history_of_the_Sikhs-p20-46)

According to historian Derryl N. Maclean, early connections between Sindh (in present-day Pakistan) and the [Shia](/wiki/Shia_Islam "Shia Islam") supporters of Ali can be traced to Hakim ibn Jabalah al-Abdi. A [companion of Muhammad](/wiki/Companions_of_the_Prophet "Companions of the Prophet"), Hakim traveled through Sind to [Makran](/wiki/Makran "Makran") in 649 CE, reporting on the region to the Caliph. A devoted supporter of Ali, Hakim died in the [Battle of the Camel](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Camel "Battle of the Camel") alongside Sindhi [Jats](/wiki/Jats "Jats").[[47]](#cite_note-47) He was also a poet and few couplets of his poem in praise of [Ali ibn Abu Talib](/wiki/Ali "Ali") have survived, as reported in Chachnama.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[a]](#cite_note-51)

During Ali's leadership, numerous Jats in Sind embraced Islam,[[51]](#cite_note-52) influenced by the efforts of figures like Harith ibn Murrah al-Abdi and Sayfi ibn Fil' al-Shaybani, officers in Ali’s army. In 658 CE, they led campaigns against Sindhi [bandits](/wiki/Banditry "Banditry"), pursuing them as far as Al-Qiqan (modern-day [Quetta](/wiki/Quetta "Quetta")).[[52]](#cite_note-53)

## Conversions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_South_Asia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Conversions")]

Main article: [Islamic missionary activity § In South Asia](/wiki/Islamic_missionary_activity#In_South_Asia "Islamic missionary activity")

The Islamic ambitions of the [sultans](/wiki/Sultans "Sultans") and [Mughals](/wiki/Mughal_dynasty "Mughal dynasty") had concentrated in expanding Muslim power and looting, not in seeking converts. Evidence of the absence of systematic programs for conversion is the reason for the concentration of South Asia's Muslim populations outside the main core of the Muslim polities[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf20026-54) in the [northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_South_Asia "Northeastern South Asia") and [northwest regions](/wiki/Northwestern_South_Asia "Northwestern South Asia") of the subcontinent, which were on the peripheries of Muslim states.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEaton1993115-55)

The Sufis did not preach egalitarianism, but played an important role in integrating agricultural settlements with the larger contemporary cultures. In areas where Sufis received grants and supervised clearing of forestry, they had the role of mediating with worldly and divine authority. [Richard M. Eaton](/wiki/Richard_M._Eaton "Richard M. Eaton") has described the significance of this in the context of [West Punjab](/wiki/West_Punjab "West Punjab") and [East Bengal](/wiki/East_Bengal "East Bengal"), the two main areas to develop Muslim majorities.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMetcalfMetcalf20027-56) The 1947 [partition](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") was eventually made possible because of the concentration of Muslim majorities in northwest and northeast India.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETalbotSingh200927-57) The overwhelming majority of the subcontinent's Muslims live in regions which became Pakistan in 1947.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEaton1993119-58)

These nominal conversions to Islam, brought about by regional Muslim polities, were followed by [reforms](/wiki/Islamic_modernism "Islamic modernism"), especially after the 17th century, in which Muslims integrated with the larger Muslim world. Improved transport services in the nineteenth century brought Muslim masses into contact with Mecca, which facilitated reformist movements stressing [Quranic literalism](/wiki/Quranic_literalism "Quranic literalism") and making people aware of the differences between Islamic commands and their actual practices.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEaton1985121-59)

Islamic reformist movements, such as the [Faraizi movement](/wiki/Faraizi_movement "Faraizi movement"), in the nineteenth century rural Bengal aimed to remove indigenous folk practices from Bengali Islam and commit the population exclusively to Allah and Muhammad.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEaton1993282-60) Politically the reform aspect of conversion, emphasizing exclusiveness, continued with the [Pakistan movement](/wiki/Pakistan_movement "Pakistan movement") for a separate Muslim state[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEaton1985121-59) and a cultural aspect was the assumption of [Arab culture](/wiki/Arab_culture "Arab culture").[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEaton1985122-61)
